name: Jarnsaxa
description: Jarnsaxa is a jötunn in Norse mythology, known primarily as one of Thor's lovers and the mother of Magni. Her name translates to "Iron Knife" or "Iron Sword," suggesting a connection to strength, weaponry, or perhaps even the harshness of the natural world. While she is not a central figure in the surviving Norse texts, her role as the mother of Magni, a god of immense strength, establishes her importance within the divine lineage. Jarnsaxa represents the untamed power of the jötnar and the potential for even giants to contribute to the Aesir line. She is a symbol of the complex relationships and interactions between the gods and the giants, highlighting the blurred lines between order and chaos in the Norse cosmos. Though her specific powers are not explicitly detailed, her association with iron and her lineage suggest a connection to strength, resilience, and the raw forces of nature. Her brief mentions in the Eddas hint at a figure worthy of respect and awe, even if her full story remains obscured by the passage of time. The nature of her relationship with Thor is also a point of interest, showing the God of Thunder engaging in relationships outside of his marriage with Sif. She embodies the chaotic and often untamed relationships between the Aesir and the Jotnar, hinting at a richer tapestry of interactions than often portrayed.
subtitle: Mother of Magni, lover of Thor

keyMyths.title: Birth of Magni
keyMyths.description: Jarnsaxa is known for giving birth to Magni, the son of Thor, who is exceptionally strong from birth. Magni helps Thor when he is trapped under a giant, showcasing his incredible strength.

keyMyths.title: Thor's Lover
keyMyths.description: Jarnsaxa is mentioned as one of Thor's lovers, highlighting the complex relationships between the gods and the giants. This relationship showcases the blending of different groups in Norse Mythology.

keyMyths.title: Mention in Names
keyMyths.description: Jarnsaxa's name is used as a kenning for shield-maidens, further solidifying her association with strength and warfare. This signifies her power and influence beyond direct narratives.

sources.author: Snorri Sturluson
